text: Transhiatal robotic esophageal epiphrenic diverticulectomy, hellermyotomy, lateral fundoplication, and couroplasty. This is the case of a sixty-eight year old male referred to thoracic surgery by his local gastroenterologist. His symptoms included dysphagia worse with solids, regurgitation, halitosis, heartburn, and retrosternal chest pain. His barium esophagram showed a large five point one by five point two centimeter epiphrenic diverticulum. Here are a few slides from his cross-sectional imaging showing the epiphenic diverticulum.

Upper GI endoscopy showed a large, clean diverticulum just above the G junction and Z line. High resolution manometry showed normal LES relaxation, zero percent bolus clearance, IRP of twenty nine point four, a resting pressure of eleven, a DCI of seven fifty three, inadequate paracelsus, and no hiatal hernia. Decision was made to proceed with surgery due to the patient's symptoms.

The surgeon divides the gastrohepatic ligament and identifies the right crus, continuing dissection into the hiatus towards the chest. The short gastric vessels on the superior fundus side are divided without fully mobilizing the greater curvature of the stomach. The left crus is identified, and the intrathoracic esophagus is dissected, revealing the diverticulum. Using blunt dissection and a vessel sealer, the diverticulum is circumferentially dissected. The esophagus is further mobilized into the chest, with care taken to protect the posterior vagus nerve.
An intraoperative upper GI endoscopy is performed, visually confirming the presence of the diverticulum above the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon uses a robotic 45mm green stapler to divide the fully mobilized intrathoracic diverticulum into the abdomen in two firings, ensuring the esophageal lumen is not impinged. The diverticulum is then removed using a 10mm bag.

The surgeon performs a lateral myotomy on the patient's right side, approximately ninety degrees away from the staple line, using Maryland bipolar forceps. The myotomy extends at least ten centimeters long and reaches well onto the stomach mucosa. An air leak test is conducted following the myotomy, which returns negative.

The surgeon performs a lateral fundoplication by approximating the fundus of the stomach to the left side of the esophagus, recreating the angle of His. A 2-0 suture is used to secure the fundoplication to the inferior staple line. An omental patch is then secured to the upper portion of the diverticulum staple line. The procedure concludes with a primary chiroplasty.

Upper GI on postoperative day one was negative for a leak or outflow obstruction. The patient was discharged on postoperative day two and on follow-up was noted to have complete resolution of preoperative symptoms with the plan to follow up as needed and with his local gastroenterologist.
